title: Storm-Damaged Tree: What to Do First in Florida
strengths: Strong safety-first intent; correctly separates active tree hazards from ordinary debris; covers exclusion, hanging wood, root-plate movement, line hazards, documentation, roof contact, delayed failure, and professional assessment.
weaknesses: No H1, authoritative sources, decision table, contextual internal links, direct emergency/service routes, current update date, 911/fire/gas/utility hierarchy, energized-ground warning, evacuation criteria, insurer notification, staged make-safe versus final cleanup, or property reopening criteria.
ai_notes: Step-by-step structure is useful but repeatedly restates that cleanup is not the first priority. Predictable sections include hidden damage, common mistake, assessment questions, what not to prioritize, checklist, and final takeaway.
florida_notes: The rewrite should become a compact first-response protocol: 911 and utility first where applicable, exclusion zone, safe documentation, insurer notice, emergency make-safe, standing-tree assessment, cleanup/removal phase, and reopening.
internal_link_opportunities: emergency safer-before-help, tree touching power lines, storm cleanup versus removal, kids and pets yard safety, delayed storm failure, insurance photos, emergency and removal services.
cta_recommendation: Add an explicit emergency hierarchy. ProTreeTrim should be contacted after 911, fire, gas, structural, and utility priorities are handled.
image_prompt: A Florida storm-damaged tree first-hour flowchart showing 911, electric utility, fire and gas, evacuation, exclusion zone, safe photos, insurer, emergency make-safe, standing-tree assessment, cleanup, removal, and yard reopening.

title: Hurricane Season in Florida: Signs a Tree May Not Make It Through the Next Major Storm
strengths: Strong seasonal search intent; covers changing lean, root-plate movement, deadwood, canopy imbalance, cracks, prior storm damage, target proximity, repeated limb drop, and early inspection.
weaknesses: No H1, authoritative hurricane/tree sources, decision table, contextual links, CTA, direct service routes, current date, named-storm stop-work rule, utility hierarchy, species-specific nuance, pruning-versus-removal decision, assessment method, monitoring triggers, or explanation that no inspection can predict hurricane survival.
ai_notes: Eight numbered signs create a repetitive template. Several sections use fear framing such as 'one storm away' and 'waiting is expensive' without enough distinction between observable defect, target exposure, and uncertain future failure.
florida_notes: The rewrite must avoid claiming that a tree can be predicted to 'make it through' a storm. It should frame risk as defect, likelihood, consequence, target, soil, recent change, and mitigation under current conditions.
internal_link_opportunities: how to tell if likely to fall, post-storm inspection, high-wind failures, saturated-soil failure, palm hurricane trimming, pruning versus removal, tree risk and emergency service routes.
cta_recommendation: Route planned pre-season inspection and pruning separately from active hazards. Add a no-last-minute-pruning warning once tropical conditions are approaching.
image_prompt: A Florida pre-hurricane tree-risk matrix showing new lean, root-plate movement, trunk crack, deadwood, canopy imbalance, old storm damage, targets, saturated soil, inspect, prune, monitor, remove, utility first, and named-storm stop-work.

title: How to Tell If a Tree Is Likely to Fall
strengths: Strong homeowner triage; prioritizes roots and soil, distinguishes new from old lean, covers cracks, conks, deadwood, root damage, targets, recent changes, power lines, DIY limits, and multiple-sign logic.
weaknesses: No H1, authoritative tree-risk sources, decision table, contextual links, direct service routes, current update date, explicit no-guarantee boundary, formal risk-assessment distinction, time frame, target occupancy, likelihood/consequence framework, emergency hierarchy, or monitoring documentation.
ai_notes: The short-answer format and sequence of sign sections are clear, but the article ends with the familiar quick check, what not to do, professional help, final takeaway, and FAQ pattern.
florida_notes: The revision should use a red/amber/green triage while explaining that homeowners can identify warning signs but cannot reliably calculate failure probability from a page or photograph.
internal_link_opportunities: leaning tree, root-plate movement, base decay, trunk cracks, conks, split tree, storm emergency, pruning versus removal, emergency and removal services.
cta_recommendation: Separate immediate exclusion/utility response from scheduled qualified assessment. Avoid implying a dispatch provider performs a formal tree-risk assessment unless credentials and scope are confirmed.
image_prompt: A Florida tree-failure warning chart with red, amber, and green lanes showing root-plate movement, new lean, trunk split, hanging branch, conks, deadwood, target exposure, monitoring, qualified assessment, utility first, and emergency exclusion zone.

title: When to Choose Tree Removal Over Pruning
strengths: Clear commercial decision intent; useful distinction between localized canopy issues and whole-tree structural problems; addresses trunk/base defects, targets, excessive pruning, recurring failures, storm damage, cost cycles, and preservation emotion.
weaknesses: No H1, sources, table, internal links, CTA, service routes, current date, cabling/monitoring/target-movement alternatives, pruning-dose and species nuance, permit/HOA/wildlife/utility screen, formal assessment boundary, or quote comparison. The slogan 'pruning solves branch problems, removal solves tree problems' is memorable but too absolute.
ai_notes: Five sign sections, emotional explanation, Florida reasons, can/cannot list, questions, common mistake, honest solution, and final takeaway create a predictable template.
florida_notes: The revision should organize the decision around defect location, target, mitigation effectiveness, pruning consequence, remaining life, monitoring feasibility, authorization, and total project scope.
internal_link_opportunities: how to tell if likely to fall, cabling and bracing, split/hollow/root-damaged tree pages, overpruning, permit and HOA pages, trimming and removal services.
cta_recommendation: Provide separate routes for preservation assessment, defined pruning, and authorized removal. Ask for two written scopes when both retention and removal remain viable.
visual_opportunity: Prune, preserve, monitor, or remove matrix.
image_prompt: A Florida tree decision matrix comparing monitor, structural pruning, cabling, target movement, and removal based on branch defect, trunk defect, root defect, target, mitigation effectiveness, permits, and long-term maintenance.

title: What Part of a Tree Should Not Be Cut?
strengths: Comprehensive practical coverage of branch collars, leaders, structural roots, root flare, palm bud, major limbs, tension-loaded wood, codominant stems, deadwood, shoots, topping, flush cuts, and professional-stop conditions.
weaknesses: No H1, authoritative pruning standards, decision table, contextual links, direct service routes, current date, species/age/objective qualifications, pruning dose, three-cut method, cut-position visuals, utility-first hierarchy, wildlife screen, or clear distinction between homeowner hand-pruning and professional work. Some statements are too universal.
ai_notes: The article is dense and list-heavy, with repeated 'should not' sections followed by extra-caution list, when cutting is right, questions, mistakes, professional help, takeaway, and FAQ.
florida_notes: The revision should become a cut-location and stop-work guide, not a list of tree parts that can never be cut. Use accepted pruning terminology and separate broadleaf trees, conifers, palms, roots, and storm-loaded material.
internal_link_opportunities: bad pruning, overpruning, palm pruning, root cutting, codominant stems, included bark, DIY trimming, power-line safety, tree trimming service.
cta_recommendation: Route small ground-level hand pruning, qualified arborist planning, utility line-clearance, and removal work separately.
image_prompt: An annotated Florida tree anatomy diagram showing branch collar, branch bark ridge, leader, codominant union, structural roots, root flare, palm bud, tension-loaded storm limb, correct cut zone, no-cut zone, and professional-only conditions.

title: If Your Tree Falls on a Neighbor’s Property in Florida, Who May Be Responsible?
strengths: Correctly begins with safety, discourages rushed admissions, emphasizes documentation and calm communication, and recognizes prior condition, foreseeability, storm circumstances, insurance, cleanup, and neighbor relations.
weaknesses: No H1, legal or insurance sources, decision table, contextual links, CTA, service routes, current date, 911/utility/structural hierarchy, insurer-notice sequence, evidence-preservation details, property-entry consent, debris/removal authorization, policy-versus-liability distinction, attorney boundary, or handling when the trunk and damage cross multiple parcels.
ai_notes: Repeated emotional setup and 'why this matters' framing. The article circles around foreseeability and avoids a precise workflow, then ends with better questions, prevention, and final takeaway.
florida_notes: High legal and insurance sensitivity. The rewrite must avoid declaring negligence or payment responsibility, explain that coverage and legal liability are separate, preserve evidence, notify affected insurers, control entry and cleanup authority, and recommend Florida legal advice for disputes or substantial loss.
internal_link_opportunities: who is responsible when neighbor tree falls, tree disputes hub, branch/roots property line, storm cleanup, insurance photos, liability page, emergency and removal services.
cta_recommendation: Primary CTA should be safety, insurer notification, evidence preservation, and legal advice where disputed. ProTreeTrim should only route authorized physical tree work.
image_prompt: A Florida neighbor-tree incident workflow showing 911 and utility, two properties, safe photos, both insurers, owner consent, emergency make-safe work, cleanup authorization, prior notices, attorney review, and authorized tree service.

florida_notes: The rewrite should avoid an unsupported statewide price range. It should teach quote drivers, information to collect, preservation/value questions, permit screening, and line-item comparison.

title: Can a Mature Tree Be Moved Instead of Removed in Florida?
strengths: Strongest article in the package; nuanced move-versus-remove-versus-replant decision; covers root loss, species, condition, equipment, access, irrigation, aftercare, permits, utilities, hardscape, survival uncertainty, relocation candidates, and good homeowner questions.
weaknesses: No H1, decision table, contextual internal links, direct service routes, current update date, linked primary sources, root-ball and preparation process boundaries, transplant-season/site preparation nuance, contracting/warranty terms, professional roles, realistic establishment monitoring, or clear statement that ordinary removal providers may not perform mature-tree relocation.
ai_notes: The short answer and many section headings are predictable, but specificity and trade-off analysis reduce AI-pattern risk. The sources are listed as plain text rather than usable links.
florida_notes: The rewrite should separate feasibility assessment, design/permit review, pre-root-pruning preparation, relocation day, planting depth, stabilization, irrigation, warranty, and long-term monitoring.
internal_link_opportunities: transplant shock, right tree/right place, root pruning, utility 811, construction compaction, removal versus pruning, tree removal service.
cta_recommendation: State that relocation requires a specialist and site-specific survival plan. ProTreeTrim can route removal/pruning but should not imply all network providers perform large-tree relocation.
visual_opportunity: Move, retain, or remove feasibility matrix.
